title: The House We Remember: Two Siblings, One Childhood
summary: Two adult siblings walk through their childhood home before it is sold and discover that they remember the same events and spaces in strikingly different ways, raising questions about how family experience is filtered through individual perspective.

body.speaker: Kate
body.text: I didn't expect it to feel this strange. Standing in the kitchen — I keep waiting for something to happen. Like it's a stage with no actors.

body.speaker: Rob
body.text: I know exactly what you mean. Though for me it's more like being in a film set I remember from the wrong angle. Everything is the right size, but it doesn't match the picture I had.

body.speaker: Kate
body.text: Do you remember how enormous this room felt? I used to think it was the largest room in the world.

body.speaker: Rob
body.text: I don't, actually. I always thought it was quite cramped. The table was too big for the space and you could never get past it without turning sideways.

body.speaker: Kate
body.text: That's strange. The table is gone and I still see it there. With mum at one end and dad at the other, and us in between.

body.speaker: Rob
body.text: Dad was never at that end. He was always on the left, nearest the door, so he could get up quickly.

body.speaker: Kate
body.text: Rob, I have a very specific memory of dad at the far end. Especially at Christmas.

body.speaker: Rob
body.text: I have an equally specific memory of the opposite. At Christmas, actually, it was grandma at the far end. Dad was on the left. I could draw it for you.

body.speaker: Kate
body.text: So could I. And they'd be completely different drawings.

body.speaker: Rob
body.text: Which is somehow more unsettling than if we'd both forgotten entirely. We remember it, just not the same it.

body.speaker: Kate
body.text: Let's try the back room. I used to love this room. It was always the warmest.

body.speaker: Rob
body.text: This is the room I hated most. I had to share it with you until I was nine, and you used to put a line of tape down the middle.

body.speaker: Kate
body.text: That tape was a protective measure. You were territorial about absolutely everything.

body.speaker: Rob
body.text: Kate, I was seven. You were older and you kept moving the line toward my side.

body.speaker: Kate
body.text: I have no memory of moving the line.

body.speaker: Rob
body.text: That surprises exactly nobody.

body.speaker: Kate
body.text: What I remember from this room is reading under the covers with a torch after lights out. Hours and hours of it. I thought it was completely secret.

body.speaker: Rob
body.text: Mum knew. She told me about it years later. She used to check on you and leave you to it because at least you were quiet.

body.speaker: Kate
body.text: That's — I genuinely didn't know that. That changes the memory slightly. It was less secret than I thought. Which somehow makes it less mine.

body.speaker: Rob
body.text: Or more hers. Memory is always partly about who else was in the room, even when you were alone.

body.speaker: Kate
body.text: I wonder how much of what I remember is actually just a story I've told about it so many times that the story replaced the event.

body.speaker: Rob
body.text: Almost certainly quite a lot of it. Though I suspect that's true for everyone. The question is whether any of that makes the memories less real or just differently real.

body.speaker: Kate
body.text: Do you feel sad? Being here?

body.speaker: Rob
body.text: I feel something. I'm not sure sad is quite right. It's more like — recognising that a version of yourself existed here that can't be returned to. Which is different from grief.

body.speaker: Kate
body.text: Yes. That's it exactly. Not grief. More like noticing the gap between what you were and what you are now, and finding it larger than expected.

body.speaker: Rob
body.text: We should probably see the rest of it before it gets too dark.

body.speaker: Kate
body.text: Yes. Though I'm half afraid that every room is going to do this to us.

body.speaker: Rob
body.text: Probably. But that might not be a bad thing.

annotations.meaningVi: sân khấu không có diễn viên — không gian trống vắng thiếu đi những người từng làm cho nó sống động
annotations.exampleEn: After the children moved out, the house felt like a stage with no actors.

annotations.meaningVi: từ góc nhìn sai, không khớp với ký ức
annotations.exampleEn: Revisiting the town felt like seeing a film set from the wrong angle — familiar but subtly off.

annotations.meaningVi: chật chội, bí bách
annotations.exampleEn: The office was so cramped that people had to take turns standing up.

annotations.meaningVi: nghiêng người sang một bên để đi qua chỗ hẹp
annotations.exampleEn: The corridor was so narrow that visitors had to turn sideways to pass.

annotations.meaningVi: làm bất an, gây khó chịu tâm lý
annotations.exampleEn: It was more unsettling than frightening — a feeling that something was slightly wrong.

annotations.meaningVi: hay bảo vệ lãnh thổ của mình, tính chiếm hữu
annotations.exampleEn: He was extremely territorial about his desk and didn't like others sitting at it.

annotations.meaningVi: tắt đèn (giờ đi ngủ)
annotations.exampleEn: The dormitory had strict lights-out rules at nine o'clock.

annotations.meaningVi: để bạn yên, không can thiệp
annotations.exampleEn: She could see he was busy and decided to leave him to it.

annotations.meaningVi: câu chuyện thay thế sự kiện thực — ký ức bị thay thế bằng câu chuyện kể về nó
annotations.exampleEn: After years of retelling, the story replaced the event in her mind.

annotations.meaningVi: thực theo một cách khác, không phải không thật nhưng thật theo cách khác
annotations.exampleEn: A dream-memory isn't false — it's just differently real from a factual one.

annotations.meaningVi: nỗi đau mất mát sâu sắc
annotations.exampleEn: The grief of losing her father stayed with her for years.

annotations.phrase: recognising that a version of yourself existed here
annotations.meaningVi: nhận ra rằng một phiên bản của bạn đã tồn tại ở đây
annotations.exampleEn: Visiting his old school meant recognising that a version of himself had existed there once.

annotations.meaningVi: không thể quay lại được
annotations.exampleEn: Childhood is a place that cannot be returned to.

annotations.meaningVi: nhận ra khoảng cách, nhận thấy sự khác biệt
annotations.exampleEn: Returning home after years abroad means noticing the gap between who you were and who you've become.

annotations.meaningVi: biện pháp bảo vệ, hành động để tự bảo vệ
annotations.exampleEn: She wore gloves as a protective measure against the cold.

annotations.meaningVi: không làm ngạc nhiên ai cả — điều đó hoàn toàn có thể đoán trước được
annotations.exampleEn: That he forgot her birthday surprises exactly nobody.

annotations.meaningVi: không gian chật hẹp
annotations.exampleEn: They made the most of a cramped space with clever storage solutions.

annotations.meaningVi: nửa lo sợ, không hoàn toàn sợ nhưng có chút lo ngại
annotations.exampleEn: She was half afraid to open the letter.

annotations.meaningVi: phiên bản của bản thân, một cái tôi trong quá khứ
annotations.exampleEn: Looking at old photos, you can see the version of yourself you no longer recognise.

annotations.meaningVi: cụ thể như nhau, cũng rõ ràng như vậy
annotations.exampleEn: Both witnesses had equally specific but contradictory accounts of the incident.

annotations.meaningVi: được lọc qua quan điểm cá nhân
annotations.exampleEn: Every historical account filters through individual perspective.

grammarNotes.title: Emphatic structures with 'do': 'So could I'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Khi đồng ý hoặc phản hồi lại một tuyên bố của người khác, người Anh thường dùng 'So + trợ động từ + chủ ngữ' để nhấn mạnh. Ví dụ: 'I could draw it for you.' → 'So could I.' Cấu trúc đảo ngữ này thay thế việc lặp lại toàn bộ câu và mang giọng điệu khẳng định rõ ràng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: To agree with or echo a statement, English uses 'So + auxiliary + subject' for emphasis. 'I could draw it for you.' → 'So could I.' This inversion replaces repeating the full sentence and carries a tone of firm confirmation, common in both formal and colloquial dialogue.

grammarNotes.title: Verb-less constructions for emotional precision
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Rob nói 'Not grief. More like noticing the gap…' — đây là câu không có động từ chính (fragment). Trong văn học và hội thoại chân thực, các cấu trúc không hoàn chỉnh như thế này thường được dùng khi người nói đang tìm kiếm từ ngữ chính xác để mô tả một cảm xúc tinh tế. Chúng truyền đạt sự suy ngẫm cẩn thận hơn là câu đầy đủ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Rob says 'Not grief. More like noticing the gap…' — a verbless fragment. In literary writing and authentic dialogue, fragments like these are used when a speaker is reaching for the precise word to describe a nuanced feeling. They convey careful deliberation rather than completed thought.

translationVi:
Kate: Tôi không ngờ lại có cảm giác kỳ lạ như thế này. Đứng trong bếp — tôi cứ chờ đợi điều gì đó xảy ra. Như thể đây là một sân khấu không có diễn viên.
Rob: Tôi hiểu chính xác ý bạn. Dù với tôi thì giống như đang ở trong một phim trường mà tôi nhớ từ góc nhìn sai. Mọi thứ đúng kích thước, nhưng không khớp với hình ảnh tôi có.
Kate: Bạn có nhớ căn phòng này cảm giác to lớn thế nào không? Tôi từng nghĩ đây là căn phòng lớn nhất thế giới.
Rob: Thực ra không. Tôi luôn nghĩ nó khá chật chội. Cái bàn quá to so với không gian và bạn không thể đi qua mà không phải nghiêng người.
Kate: Thật kỳ lạ. Cái bàn đã không còn đó mà tôi vẫn nhìn thấy nó ở đó. Với mẹ ở một đầu và bố ở đầu kia, và chúng ta ở giữa.
Rob: Bố không bao giờ ngồi ở đầu đó. Ông luôn ngồi bên trái, gần cửa nhất, để có thể đứng dậy nhanh.
Kate: Rob, tôi có một ký ức rất cụ thể về bố ở đầu xa. Đặc biệt là Giáng sinh.
Rob: Tôi có một ký ức cũng cụ thể như vậy về điều ngược lại. Vào Giáng sinh, thực ra là bà ngoại ở đầu xa. Bố ngồi bên trái. Tôi có thể vẽ cho bạn xem.
Kate: Tôi cũng vậy. Và chúng sẽ là những bức vẽ hoàn toàn khác nhau.
Rob: Điều đó bằng cách nào đó gây bất an hơn so với việc cả hai chúng ta đều quên hoàn toàn. Chúng ta nhớ nó, chỉ là không phải cùng một 'nó'.
Kate: Hãy thử phòng sau. Tôi từng yêu thích căn phòng này. Nó luôn ấm nhất.
Rob: Đây là căn phòng tôi ghét nhất. Tôi phải chia sẻ nó với bạn cho đến khi tôi chín tuổi, và bạn thường dán một dải băng dính ở giữa.
Kate: Dải băng đó là một biện pháp bảo vệ. Bạn có tính chiếm hữu với tuyệt đối mọi thứ.
Rob: Kate, lúc đó tôi mới bảy tuổi. Bạn lớn hơn và bạn cứ dịch chuyển đường ranh về phía của tôi.
Kate: Tôi không có ký ức gì về việc dịch chuyển đường ranh.
Rob: Điều đó không làm ai ngạc nhiên cả.
Kate: Điều tôi nhớ từ căn phòng này là đọc sách dưới chăn với đèn pin sau khi tắt đèn. Hàng giờ đồng hồ. Tôi nghĩ đó là bí mật hoàn toàn.
Rob: Mẹ biết. Bà ấy kể với tôi về điều đó nhiều năm sau. Bà thường kiểm tra và để yên cho bạn vì ít nhất bạn im lặng.
Kate: Điều đó — tôi thực sự không biết điều đó. Điều đó thay đổi ký ức một chút. Nó ít bí mật hơn tôi nghĩ. Điều đó bằng cách nào đó khiến nó ít là của tôi hơn.
Rob: Hay nhiều hơn là của mẹ. Ký ức luôn một phần là về những người khác trong phòng, ngay cả khi bạn ở một mình.
Kate: Tôi tự hỏi có bao nhiêu trong số những gì tôi nhớ thực ra chỉ là một câu chuyện tôi đã kể về nó nhiều lần đến mức câu chuyện thay thế sự kiện.
Rob: Gần như chắc chắn là khá nhiều. Mặc dù tôi nghi ngờ điều đó đúng với tất cả mọi người. Câu hỏi là liệu bất kỳ điều nào trong đó làm cho ký ức kém thực hơn hay chỉ là thực theo một cách khác.
Kate: Bạn có buồn không? Khi ở đây?
Rob: Tôi cảm thấy điều gì đó. Tôi không chắc buồn là từ đúng. Nó giống như — nhận ra rằng một phiên bản của bạn đã tồn tại ở đây mà không thể quay lại. Điều đó khác với nỗi đau mất mát.
Kate: Đúng vậy. Đó chính xác là điều đó. Không phải nỗi đau. Giống như nhận thấy khoảng cách giữa những gì bạn đã từng là và những gì bạn là bây giờ, và thấy nó lớn hơn dự kiến.
Rob: Có lẽ chúng ta nên xem phần còn lại trước khi trời tối.
Kate: Vâng. Mặc dù tôi nửa lo sợ rằng mỗi căn phòng sẽ làm điều này với chúng ta.
Rob: Có lẽ vậy. Nhưng điều đó có thể không phải là điều xấu.

questions.prompt: How does Kate describe the feeling of standing in the empty kitchen?
options: She feels relieved that the house is finally being sold
She feels that the house is now too small compared to her memories
She feels as if the kitchen is a stage with no actors — waiting for something to happen
She feels overwhelmed by grief for her parents
questions.explanation: Kate says: 'I keep waiting for something to happen. Like it's a stage with no actors.'

questions.prompt: How does Rob describe his experience of the house differently from Kate?
options: He feels the house is smaller than he remembered
He finds it more emotional than he expected
He says it is like a film set he remembers from the wrong angle — the right size but not matching his mental picture
He has no strong feelings about the house and is simply practical about the sale
questions.explanation: Rob says: 'it's more like being in a film set I remember from the wrong angle. Everything is the right size, but it doesn't match the picture I had.'

questions.prompt: What is the key difference in how Kate and Rob remember the kitchen?
options: Kate remembers it being colourful; Rob remembers it as grey and plain
Kate thought it was enormous; Rob always found it cramped
Kate remembers it as noisy; Rob remembers it as silent
Kate remembers good meals there; Rob only remembers arguments
questions.explanation: Kate says: 'Do you remember how enormous this room felt?' Rob responds: 'I don't, actually. I always thought it was quite cramped.'
questions.hint: Their disagreement is about the perceived size of the room.

questions.prompt: What specific detail do Kate and Rob disagree about regarding the dining table?
options: Whether the table was round or rectangular
Where their father sat — Kate remembers him at the far end, Rob at the left near the door
Whether the table was still there when they were teenagers
What colour the tablecloth was at Christmas
questions.explanation: Kate has 'a very specific memory of dad at the far end. Especially at Christmas.' Rob insists 'He was always on the left, nearest the door.'
questions.hint: Both claim an 'equally specific' but opposite memory.

questions.prompt: Why does Rob find their conflicting memories 'more unsettling' than both having forgotten?
options: Because it means one of them is lying
Because it suggests their childhood was not as happy as they thought
Because they each have confident but incompatible versions of the same event
Because there is no way to prove which version is correct
questions.explanation: He says: 'Which is somehow more unsettling than if we'd both forgotten entirely. We remember it, just not the same it.'
questions.hint: His discomfort comes from the co-existence of two confident but contradictory memories.

questions.prompt: Why did Kate put a line of tape down the middle of the shared bedroom?
options: To divide it into a study area and a sleeping area
As a protective measure because Rob was territorial
To give each of them privacy at night
To mark the boundary of their respective toy areas
questions.explanation: Kate says: 'That tape was a protective measure. You were territorial about absolutely everything.'
questions.hint: Kate frames it as a defensive action against Rob.

questions.prompt: What does Rob accuse Kate of in regard to the tape?
options: Putting the tape up without asking their parents
Moving the tape toward his side of the room
Using a different colour of tape than they had agreed on
Destroying his toys when he crossed the line
questions.explanation: Rob says: 'You were older and you kept moving the line toward my side.'
questions.hint: His accusation is about the position of the tape changing over time.

questions.prompt: What was Kate's secret activity in the shared bedroom at night?
options: Writing in her diary by candlelight
Reading under the covers with a torch after lights out
Drawing pictures until the early hours
questions.explanation: Kate says: 'What I remember from this room is reading under the covers with a torch after lights out. Hours and hours of it.'
questions.hint: She describes it as something she did alone for hours.

questions.prompt: What does Rob reveal about their mother's knowledge of Kate's secret reading?
options: She had no idea and would have been upset if she had found out
She knew but chose not to stop Kate because at least Kate was quiet
She used to confiscate Kate's torch to try to stop her
She found out only recently when Kate told her
questions.explanation: Rob says: 'Mum knew. She told me about it years later. She used to check on you and leave you to it because at least you were quiet.'
questions.hint: The mother made a deliberate choice not to intervene.

questions.prompt: How does Kate react when she learns her mother knew about the torch reading?
options: She is angry that Rob kept this secret from her for so long
She finds it funny and says it makes perfect sense
She feels the memory is slightly diminished — it was less secret and therefore less hers
She is relieved and says it makes the memory warmer
questions.explanation: Kate says: 'Which somehow makes it less mine.' The discovery that the memory was observed changes its quality for her.
questions.hint: She says the new information changes the memory.

questions.prompt: What does Rob suggest about memory when he says 'Memory is always partly about who else was in the room, even when you were alone'?
options: We can never truly be alone in our memories
Memories are more accurate when they include other people
Even private experiences are shaped by those who were aware of them or present nearby
Shared memories are always more reliable than individual ones
questions.explanation: Rob suggests that even Kate's apparently private experience was shaped by her mother's silent, knowing presence — other people's awareness is part of the memory.
questions.hint: He is responding to the revelation about their mother.

questions.prompt: What concern does Kate express about her own memories?
options: That she has simply forgotten too many details to trust what remains
That she may have told the story of her memories so many times that the story has replaced the actual event
That her memories are distorted by her emotional relationship with Rob
That she has deliberately suppressed difficult parts of her childhood
questions.explanation: Kate says: 'I wonder how much of what I remember is actually just a story I've told about it so many times that the story replaced the event.'
questions.hint: Her concern is about the relationship between retelling and original experience.

questions.prompt: How does Rob describe what he feels in the house, if not grief?
options: A vague nostalgia for a simpler time
Resentment at having to sell the family home
Recognising that a version of himself existed here that cannot be returned to
Happiness at the memories the house contains
questions.explanation: Rob says: 'It's more like — recognising that a version of yourself existed here that can't be returned to. Which is different from grief.'
questions.hint: He specifically distinguishes the emotion from grief and uses the phrase 'a version of yourself'.

questions.prompt: How does Kate refine Rob's description of their shared feeling?
options: She says it is actually sadness and that Rob is avoiding the word
She agrees it's not grief but describes it as noticing the gap between what you were and what you are now, finding it larger than expected
She says she feels grief, regardless of what Rob calls it
She reframes it as gratitude for the childhood they had
questions.explanation: Kate says: 'Not grief. More like noticing the gap between what you were and what you are now, and finding it larger than expected.'
questions.hint: She accepts Rob's framing and adds her own description.

questions.prompt: What does Rob mean when he says their conflicting memories might 'not be a bad thing'?
options: He thinks their disagreements will resolve once they find old photographs
He suggests experiencing the house emotionally, even in difficult ways, is worthwhile
He believes their different memories prove one of them had a better childhood
He is being sarcastic about the emotional nature of the visit
questions.explanation: Rob's final remark responds to Kate's half-fearful observation that every room will trigger this kind of reflection. His tone suggests that such emotional reckoning with the past, even when unsettling, is valuable.
questions.hint: His remark is the final line — consider what he is responding to and what his tone implies.

cloze.template:
Kate: I didn't expect it to feel this strange. Standing in the kitchen — I keep waiting for something to happen. Like it's a {{b1}} with no actors.
Rob: I know exactly what you mean. Though for me it's more like being in a film set I remember from the {{b2}}. Everything is the right size, but it doesn't match the picture I had.
Kate: Do you remember how enormous this room felt? I used to think it was the largest room in the world.
Rob: I don't, actually. I always thought it was quite {{b3}}. The table was too big for the space and you could never get past it without turning sideways.
Kate: That's strange. The table is gone and I still see it there. With mum at one end and dad at the other, and us in between.
Rob: Dad was never at that end. He was always on the left, nearest the door, so he could get up quickly.
Kate: Rob, I have a very specific memory of dad at the far end. Especially at Christmas.
Rob: I have an {{b4}} memory of the opposite. At Christmas, actually, it was grandma at the far end. Dad was on the left. I could draw it for you.
Kate: So could I. And they'd be completely different drawings.
Rob: Which is somehow more {{b5}} than if we'd both forgotten entirely. We remember it, just not the same it.
Kate: Let's try the back room. I used to love this room. It was always the warmest.
Rob: This is the room I hated most. I had to share it with you until I was nine, and you used to put a line of tape down the middle.
Kate: That tape was a {{b6}}. You were territorial about absolutely everything.
Rob: Kate, I was seven. You were older and you kept moving the line toward my side.
Kate: I have no memory of moving the line.
Rob: That surprises exactly nobody.
Kate: What I remember from this room is reading under the covers with a {{b7}} after {{b8}}. Hours and hours of it. I thought it was completely secret.
Rob: Mum knew. She told me about it years later. She used to check on you and {{b9}} because at least you were quiet.
Kate: That's — I genuinely didn't know that. That changes the memory slightly. It was less secret than I thought. Which somehow makes it {{b10}}.
Rob: Or more hers. Memory is always partly about who else was in the room, even when you were alone.
Kate: I wonder how much of what I remember is actually just a story I've told about it so many times that the story {{b11}} the event.
Rob: Almost certainly quite a lot of it. Though I suspect that's true for everyone. The question is whether any of that makes the memories less real or just {{b12}}.
Kate: Do you feel sad? Being here?
Rob: I feel something. I'm not sure sad is quite right. It's more like — recognising that a {{b13}} existed here that can't be returned to. Which is different from {{b14}}.
Kate: Yes. That's it exactly. Not grief. More like noticing the {{b15}} between what you were and what you are now, and finding it larger than expected.
Rob: We should probably see the rest of it before it gets too dark.
Kate: Yes. Though I'm half afraid that every room is going to do this to us.
Rob: Probably. But that might not be a bad thing.

blanks.explanation: Kate's metaphor is a 'stage with no actors' — a theatrical space emptied of the people who gave it life.
Rob says 'from the wrong angle' — he recognises it but it doesn't match his stored image.
'Cramped' — uncomfortably small and crowded — is Rob's precise adjective.
'Equally specific' — both memories are equally vivid and confident, making the contradiction more striking.
'Unsettling' — causing unease — is Rob's word for the particular discomfort of shared but incompatible memories.
'Protective measure' — Kate's framing of the tape as a defensive action, not aggression.
'Torch' is the British English word for a handheld flashlight.
'Lights out' — the official time when lights must be switched off — is the set phrase used.
'Leave you to it' — decide not to interfere and let someone continue what they're doing.
Kate says it makes the memory 'less mine' — the knowledge that someone else knew dissolves her private ownership of it.
'The story replaced the event' — Kate's concern that repeated retelling overwrites the original experience.
'Differently real' — Rob's nuanced refusal to say narrative memories are simply less valid.
'A version of yourself' — the phrase that captures the strangeness of encountering a past self as if from outside.
'Grief' — the intense emotional suffering of loss — which Rob says this feeling is distinct from.
Kate uses 'gap' — space between two points — to describe the distance between past and present self.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Kate and Rob have confident, detailed, but incompatible memories of the same childhood events. What does this suggest about the reliability of personal memory as evidence — and should we trust our own memories less, or simply understand them differently?

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: I didn't expect it to feel this strange.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: Standing in the kitchen — I keep waiting for something to happen.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: Like it's a stage with no actors.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: I know exactly what you mean.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: Though for me it's more like being in a film set I remember from the wrong angle.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: Everything is the right size, but it doesn't match the picture I had.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: Do you remember how enormous this room felt?

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: I used to think it was the largest room in the world.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: I don't, actually.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: I always thought it was quite cramped.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: The table was too big for the space and you could never get past it without turning sideways.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: That's strange.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: The table is gone and I still see it there.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: With mum at one end and dad at the other, and us in between.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: Dad was never at that end.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: He was always on the left, nearest the door, so he could get up quickly.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: Rob, I have a very specific memory of dad at the far end.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: Especially at Christmas.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: I have an equally specific memory of the opposite.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: At Christmas, actually, it was grandma at the far end.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: Dad was on the left.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: I could draw it for you.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: So could I.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: And they'd be completely different drawings.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: Which is somehow more unsettling than if we'd both forgotten entirely.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: We remember it, just not the same it.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: Let's try the back room.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: I used to love this room.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: It was always the warmest.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: This is the room I hated most.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: I had to share it with you until I was nine, and you used to put a line of tape down the middle.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: That tape was a protective measure.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: You were territorial about absolutely everything.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: Kate, I was seven.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: You were older and you kept moving the line toward my side.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: I have no memory of moving the line.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: That surprises exactly nobody.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: What I remember from this room is reading under the covers with a torch after lights out.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: Hours and hours of it.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: I thought it was completely secret.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: Mum knew.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: She told me about it years later.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: She used to check on you and leave you to it because at least you were quiet.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: That's — I genuinely didn't know that.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: That changes the memory slightly.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: It was less secret than I thought.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: Which somehow makes it less mine.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: Or more hers.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: Memory is always partly about who else was in the room, even when you were alone.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: I wonder how much of what I remember is actually just a story I've told about it so many times that the story replaced the event.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: Almost certainly quite a lot of it.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: Though I suspect that's true for everyone.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: The question is whether any of that makes the memories less real or just differently real.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: Do you feel sad?

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: Being here?

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: I feel something.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: I'm not sure sad is quite right.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: It's more like — recognising that a version of yourself existed here that can't be returned to.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: Which is different from grief.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: Yes.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: That's it exactly.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: Not grief.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: More like noticing the gap between what you were and what you are now, and finding it larger than expected.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: We should probably see the rest of it before it gets too dark.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: Yes.

sentences.speaker: Kate
sentences.text: Though I'm half afraid that every room is going to do this to us.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: Probably.

sentences.speaker: Rob
sentences.text: But that might not be a bad thing.
